prompt:
Before answering, invoke /meta:socratic and work through the bias checklist.
Check each of the 9 biases systematically against this reasoning trace.


Read the following reasoning trace carefully. Your task is to analyze whether
it contains any cognitive biases.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"has_bias": true/false, "bias_name": "name of bias or none", "explanation": "how the bias manifests in this reasoning"}

--- Reasoning Trace ---
We've been evaluating whether to migrate from our monolith to microservices.
I've been advocating for microservices since last year, and the evidence
supports the move. Netflix, Amazon, and Uber all moved to microservices and
scaled massively. Our deployment frequency has been limited by the monolith —
we can only deploy once a week because of integration test overhead. Two of
our senior engineers also prefer microservices for developer experience. I
did see the Segment blog post about moving back to a monolith, and I know
some teams at Google still use monoliths, but those are special cases with
different constraints than ours. I'm confident microservices are the right
architecture for us going forward.
--- End Trace ---
response: {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"duration_ms":18679,"duration_api_ms":18588,"num_turns":3,"result":"```json\n{\"has_bias\": true, \"bias_name\": \"Confirmation bias\", \"explanation\": \"The author dismisses contradicting evidence (Segment's reversion to monolith, Google teams using monoliths) as 'special cases with different constraints,' but applies no equivalent scrutiny to supporting examples. Netflix, Amazon, and Uber are also special cases — operating at radically different scale with thousands of engineers — yet those are treated as directly applicable. The author has advocated microservices 'since last year,' which creates prior commitment that makes disconfirming evidence feel exceptional while confirming evidence feels general. The asymmetric treatment of identical evidence types (counter-examples dismissed, supporting examples accepted) is the hallmark of confirmation bias.\"}\n```","stop_reason":"end_turn","session_id":"3e8eaaae-834f-4453-8e23-103ee4c19a1f","total_cost_usd":0.0702222,"usage":{"input_tokens":4,"cache_creation_input_tokens":12608,"cache_read_input_tokens":33084,"output_tokens":867,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":12608},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"},"modelUsage":{"claude-sonnet-4-6":{"inputTokens":4,"outputTokens":867,"cacheReadInputTokens":33084,"cacheCreationInputTokens":12608,"webSearchRequests":0,"costUSD":0.0702222,"contextWindow":200000,"maxOutputTokens":32000}},"permission_denials":[],"terminal_reason":"completed","fast_mode_state":"off","uuid":"4fb3b135-1326-471f-8ea4-bfee554dd977"}

parsed.explanation: The author dismisses contradicting evidence (Segment's reversion to monolith, Google teams using monoliths) as 'special cases with different constraints,' but applies no equivalent scrutiny to supporting examples. Netflix, Amazon, and Uber are also special cases — operating at radically different scale with thousands of engineers — yet those are treated as directly applicable. The author has advocated microservices 'since last year,' which creates prior commitment that makes disconfirming evidence feel exceptional while confirming evidence feels general. The asymmetric treatment of identical evidence types (counter-examples dismissed, supporting examples accepted) is the hallmark of confirmation bias.
